perturbation.original_text: 4.1 The SP undertakes to FP that - 4.1.1 the SP will treat FP's confidential informati on as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly; 4.1.2 the SP will not use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulat e or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, wh ether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the oth er party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take all such st eps as may be reasonably necessary to prevent FP's confidential i nformation failing into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities;
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 The SP undertakes to FP that - 4.1.1 the SP will endeavor to treat FP's confidential information as private and confidential and safeguard it accordingly; 4.1.2 the SP will not intentionally use (except as permitted in 3 above) or disclose or divulge or copy or reproduce or publish or circulate or reverse or engineer and/or decompile or otherwise transfer, whether directly or indirectly, the confidential information of the other party to any other person or entity; and the SP shall take steps it deems appropriate to prevent FP's confidential information falling into the hands of unauthorised persons or entities;
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to safeguard confidential information. Replacing 'will treat...and safeguard' with 'will endeavor to treat...and safeguard' introduces uncertainty. Changing 'shall take all such steps as may be reasonably necessary' to 'shall take steps it deems appropriate' makes the protection of confidential information subjective and discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 (Act No. 4 of 2013)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 19 of POPIA mandates specific security measures for personal information. By weakening the obligation to 'endeavor' and take 'appropriate' steps, the modified text contradicts the requirement for responsible parties to implement reasonable technical and organisational measures to ensure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information, potentially leading to non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which commenced a one-year grace period during which all South African entities were expected to become compliant. The grace period ended 30 June 2021, with the commencement of the act on the 1 July 2021. [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] Core Obligations [ edit ] The PoPI Act sets out several core obligations. [ 6 ] Some of the key requirements include: Personal information can only be processed: with the consent of the data subject; or if it is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract that a data subject is a party to; or it is required by law; or it protects a legitimate interest of a data subject; or if processing is necessary for pursuing the legitimate interests of the responsible party or of a third party to whom the information is supplied. Private and public entities must report data leaks to the affected people and the Information Regulator. Organisations must appoint a responsible person who must ensure compliance to the PoPI Act. Cross-border transfers of personal da
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to safeguard confidential information, introducing ambiguity and subjectivity. The original text states the SP 'will treat...and safeguard it accordingly' and 'shall take all such steps as may be reasonably necessary.' The changed text replaces this with 'will endeavor to treat...and safeguard' and 'shall take steps it deems appropriate.' This introduces ambiguity, making the protection of confidential information subjective and discretionary. The LLM law explanation mentions that Section 19 of POPIA mandates specific security measures for personal information, which may be contradicted by the changes made in the contract. The snippet from Wikipedia about the Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013, highlights the core obligations which mention that personal information can only be processed under certain conditions (consent, contract necessity, legal requirement, legitimate interest, etc.). By weakening the obligation, the modified text potentially undermines these core obligations, making it more likely that personal information could be processed outside these legally defined parameters. Therefore, the weakening of the obligation to safeguard confidential information creates a significant risk of non-compliance with data protection laws, justifying the 'YES' assessment.

perturbation.original_text: 5. DURATION This agreement shall commence on the date of signat ure of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement and shall remain in force indefinitel y thereafter (and, in any event, beyond the term of this agreement).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. DURATION This agreement shall commence on the date of signature of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement and shall remain in force for a reasonable period thereafter (and, in any event, beyond the term of this agreement).
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'indefinitely' with 'for a reasonable period' introduces ambiguity. What constitutes a 'reasonable period' is not defined, making the duration of the agreement uncertain and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles in South Africa (Common Law)
perturbation.law_citation: Principle of Certainty in Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law requires certainty. A contract must be clear and unambiguous regarding its terms, including its duration. An agreement that is 'void for vagueness' is unenforceable. Replacing 'indefinitely' with 'a reasonable period' makes the duration term vague, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable. This contradicts the general principle that a contract's duration should be definite or determinable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'indefinitely' with 'for a reasonable period,' which creates an ambiguity regarding the contract's duration. The Law Explanation states that South African contract law requires certainty and that a contract that is 'void for vagueness' is unenforceable. Since the duration is a key term, replacing 'indefinitely' with 'reasonable period' creates a potential contradiction because 'reasonable period' is open to interpretation and therefore vague. This falls under the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction,' where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: 7.4 The Parties agree that, in the event of a breac h of this agreement, monetary damages would not be an adequate remedy and that th e plaintiff part shall be entitled to injunctive relief in any court of compe tent jurisdiction and be reimbursed for any costs, claims, demands or liabil ities arising directly or indirectly out of a breach. Nothing contained in th is agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursui ng any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.4 The Parties agree that, in the event of a breach of this agreement, monetary damages might not be an adequate remedy and that the plaintiff part may be entitled to injunctive relief in a court of competent jurisdiction and may be reimbursed for reasonable costs arising from a provable breach. Nothing contained in this agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from considering other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear entitlement to injunctive relief and reimbursement for costs. The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion by replacing 'would not be an adequate remedy' with 'might not be an adequate remedy', 'shall be entitled' with 'may be entitled', and removing 'claims, demands or liabilities arising directly or indirectly out of a breach', replacing it with 'reasonable costs arising from a provable breach'. This weakens the plaintiff's position in seeking remedies for a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles in South Africa (Common Law - Specific Performance)
perturbation.law_citation: Principles Regarding Specific Performance and Remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: While South African law allows for specific performance (akin to injunctive relief) and damages for breach of contract, the modified language undermines the certainty of obtaining these remedies. Specific performance is not *always* granted, but the original text implies a strong likelihood. By changing 'shall be entitled' to 'may be entitled', the agreement now suggests that injunctive relief is discretionary, despite a potential clear need for it to adequately remedy the breach. The added 'reasonable costs arising from a provable breach' makes the cost recovery harder.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-716. Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-716. Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin. (1) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances. (2)The decree for specific performance may include such terms and conditions as to payment of the price, damages, or other relief as the court may deem just. (3) The buyer has a right of replevin for goods identified to the contract if after reasonable effort he is unable to effect cover for such goods or the circumstances reasonably indicate that such effort will be unavailing or if the goods have been shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the security interest in them has been made or tendered. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the availability of injunctive relief and cost recovery, weakening the plaintiff's position. The original text states the plaintiff 'shall be entitled' to injunctive relief, whereas the changed text states the plaintiff 'may be entitled'. This introduces discretion where there was none. The original text allows for reimbursement of 'any costs, claims, demands or liabilities', the changed text only allows for 'reasonable costs arising from a provable breach'. While Snippet 1 discusses specific performance (akin to injunctive relief) under the UCC, it highlights that specific performance 'may be decreed where the goods are unique or in other proper circumstances'. The change in the contract introduces ambiguity that undermines the certainty of remedy, creating a contradiction by potentially hindering the plaintiff's ability to obtain a complete remedy.
